intro_text: An argument breaks out in the Clan over the best way to store fresh-kill for leaf-bare. As a mediator, you step in to settle the dispute.
decline_text: Deciding the matter is too complex for a single cat to handle, you suggest forming a small committee to make the decision. The Clan appreciates your wise approach.

success_outcomes.text: You gather everycat's input and propose a plan that takes into account the various perspectives. The Clan agrees on your plan, commending your mediation skills.

Knowing you would be cast aside in this particular case, you sit back and listen to the argument carefully, contemplating all the logic. When it seems like the flame finally died out, you propose a plan that uses the various perspectives to the Clan's advantage. No one seems to object.

fail_outcomes.text: Despite your efforts, the Clan can't reach an agreement. l_n eventually has to step in and make a decision. This experience is a reminder that some disputes are hard to mediate.

You try your best to calm the dispute but the cats are adamant that as a mediator, you do not know nearly enough about storing fresh-kill to step into the argument. You're essentially ignored, and r_w has to step in before the argument brews further.

Though the argument only seems to be about the differencing opinions of storing fresh-kill, it intensifies far too quickly. You find yourself suspicious about the underlining cause. However, before you're able to dig deeper, the argument is settled with a sharp statement from r_w. Interesting.

intro_text: Two warriors have a heated argument about a recent hunting trip. You decide to mediate before things get out of hand.
decline_text: Feeling this situation is beyond your skill, you ask a senior warrior to intervene. Watching them handle the situation, you pick up some valuable lessons.

success_outcomes.text: Using your mediation skills, you manage to diffuse the argument. Both warriors calm down, acknowledging your fair judgement.

You suddenly remember something your former mentor taught you when you first started out as a mediator apprentice - don't ever get in between two agitated warriors. Keeping this in mind, you speed over to the closest trusted warrior and ask them to get involved instead.

Exasperated, you stroll over to the warriors and ask what is going on. They both seem to have different issues with the patrol that just transpired, but you're able to figure out that it's just petty arguing caused by being alone together. The only thing you can do is ask the deputy to not assign them together for a time.

fail_outcomes.text: Despite your best efforts, the argument escalates. A senior warrior has to intervene. Not every situation can be easily mediated, but you learn from the experience.

Certainly, the warriors are mature enough to handle their own issues - you're too busy to mediate petty fights. Later, you realize you messed up as you hear from the grapevine that two warriors got into a claw fight and ended up in the medicine den... Whoops...

With much hesitance, you try to mediate the situation, but it seems like your nervous behavior made it worse. In no time at all, the warrior pounce on each other and you're forced to find a cat stronger than you to pull them a part. You don't notice you're bleeding until after the fight ends.

intro_text: A dispute breaks out in the Clan about which part of the territory is best for hunting. As a mediator, you try to keep the peace.
decline_text: You decide to consult with some senior warriors before stepping in, believing their experience will help you mediate better. The Clan appreciates your cautious approach.

success_outcomes.text: You hear both sides and suggest a compromise that satisfies everycat. Your skills as a mediator are commended by the Clan.

Noticing that the core of the argument is that they keep talking over each other, you propose a solution. Whoever holds the feather is the only one that can talk, and the feather will be passed to the next cat when you say so. It worked splendidly. Sometimes all you have to do is treat full grown cats like kittens.

fail_outcomes.text: Despite your best efforts, the dispute continues. A senior warrior has to intervene. It's a learning experience about how hard mediation can sometimes be.

Unfortunately, you don't believe it's your place to try to mediate something so dependent on the deputy and you hesitate. It appears you hesitate for too long, and before you know it, the warriors are doing it their own way. Whether that is a good or bad thing is up to interpretation...

intro_text: The apprentices are arguing about who is the fastest runner. You decide to step in before the argument escalates.
decline_text: You decide to let the apprentices settle the argument themselves, observing how they handle the situation. Sometimes, stepping back is the best way to mediate.

success_outcomes.text: You suggest a friendly race to determine the fastest runner. The apprentices agree, the argument is settled, and everycat enjoys the race.
The argument is so mediocre that it was resolved with a simple snap to halt the argument. The apprentices huff and grumble, but they lower their voices and drop the debate... for now.

fail_outcomes.text: Despite your suggestion of a friendly race, the argument continues. It takes a while for the situation to calm down, but you learn some valuable lessons in mediation.

You brush off the argument as two apprentices just having a heated, but friendly, debate and don't think much of it. However, you notice that perhaps they're too competitive and later find that they drove themselves into exhaustion trying to prove that they're better. Oh, apprentices...

intro_text: A disagreement over border markings with a neighbouring Clan is causing tension. As a mediator, you decide to intervene.
decline_text: Feeling that the issue is too sensitive for you to handle alone, you consult with the Clan leader before taking any action. The Clan appreciates your wise decision.

success_outcomes.text: You manage to resolve the disagreement by proposing a shared patrol to remark the border. Both Clans appreciate your diplomacy and fairness.

fail_outcomes.text: Despite your efforts, the disagreement escalates into a skirmish. It's a hard lesson in the limits of mediation.

intro_text: Despite your leader barring you from doing your job as a mediator, two Clanmates stalk up to you one day, tails twitching. They ask you for your thoughts on a disagreement they've been having, and you wonder whether they really value your opinion.
decline_text: You turn them away. Your Clan has made it very clear that they don't see you as equal-- why should you solve their problems for them?

success_outcomes.text: You decide to listen to your Clanmates' problem, and once they've begun to see each other's point of view, you ask why they chose to come to you, given your situation. They say that they still respect your opinion as a mediator, despite everything, and you can't help but feel some fondness for the both of them.

fail_outcomes.text: When you offer a solution that one warrior doesn't seem to agree with, they scoff and wonder aloud why they went to a murderer to solve their problems. When they stalk away, the other half of the argument follows them, and you just stare after them regretfully.

intro_text: You watch a hunting patrol return, anticipating a good hunt. At the end of the patrol an apprentice is trudging behind with their head low.
decline_text: Everycat has a hard day, but they can rest now. It's time for you to tuck into a well deserved meal!

success_outcomes.text: As the apprentice stays on the side lines as the Clan shares tongues and freshkill, you pick up a juicy bit of prey to bring over to them. It doesn't take much for them to open up about how worried they are about their training progress. Nor does it take much to soothe their fears and reassure them that they are still learning. By the time the prey is gone the apprentice's spirits are high again as they head to their nest.

fail_outcomes.text: As you attempt to bring prey over to the lone apprentice their mentor interrupts. They tell you not to worry about their apprentice too much - they merely lost a chance to show off to the rest of the patrol. It leaves you a little unsettled but you take their word for it before tucking into your meal.

intro_text: Not every mentor and apprentice pair work well together. You take careful note of the tension between the two and gently make your way between to diffuse the situation.
decline_text: You have another matter to deal with at the moment but this can't get out of paw. You suggest that they spend the day on separate duties and attend to more pressing problems as they walk away.

success_outcomes.text: You ask to speak to the mentor alone. The conversation that follows is promising - they agree to try being more understanding with their apprentice, and attempt a different approach to teaching. You part ways with your head held high about a job well done.

You ask to speak to the apprentice alone. The conversation that follows is promising - they agree to try listening more to their mentor, and attempt to focus more on their training. You part ways with your head held high about a job well done.

You sit to speak with the pair. It is no simple matter to parse through with misunderstandings on both sides. With some effort and careful words, however, you bridge the gap and the two have reached understanding. Over the next few days you watch them work together better than ever with a smile on your face.

fail_outcomes.text: You ask to speak to the mentor alone. The conversation that follows is disheartening - they rant on and on about their upstart apprentice. There is no chance to speak through it peacefully. You part ways with your spirits low, wondering what the next step should be.

You ask to speak to the apprentice alone. The conversation that follows is disheartening - they are swiftly upset by your efforts to speak to them about listening to their mentor. Tail lashing, the apprentice sulks off to their den as their mentor calls after them crossly. You part ways with your spirits low, wondering what the next step should be.

You sit to speak with the pair. It is no simple matter to parse through with misunderstandings on both sides. No matter your efforts, the situation is becoming increasingly more heated. The leader is forced to step in and ultimately is contemplating separating the two. This is a good lesson that you can't mend every relationship.

intro_text: d_n is discussing recent border patrols with l_n - apparently some outsiders keep trespassing on c_n territory. l_n, noticing your presence nearby, asks you if you have any ideas for handling the conflict.
decline_text: You tell l_n that you don't have any ideas; you trust that {PRONOUN/l_n/object} and d_n can handle the conflict efficiently.

success_outcomes.text: You offer to join a patrol and talk to these cats yourself - surely mediators can handle conflict with cats outside Clans, too. l_n and d_n are wary, unsure if non-Clan cats will respect a Clan mediator, but they both agree that your skills are strong enough that it's worth a try. Satisfied, you and d_n begin talking between yourselves to figure out the patrol logistics.

You ask d_n how a typical interaction with these cats goes, and as {PRONOUN/d_n/subject} describes the latest encounters, you quickly realize that the patrols are inadvertently antagonizing the outsiders. The clearer picture allows you, l_n, and d_n to come up with instructions for how future patrols should interact with these cats.

The outsiders keep coming back to c_n territory because there's something they need - food, shelter, herbs, or so forth. Maybe, you suggest, the patrols could ask what the outsiders need and help them find it elsewhere. Both d_n and l_n approve of your idea, and begin discussing instructions for the next patrol that runs across them.

You ask if the outsiders might be open to joining the Clan - after all, they can't encroach on c_n territory if they're c_n cats. Though d_n and l_n both seem skeptical, they agree to at least try inviting these cats into c_n the next time a patrol sees them.

fail_outcomes.text: Great minds think alike, much to your annoyance. Every suggestion you make, d_n tells you the patrols have already tried without success. Eventually, you slink away in defeat, hoping that d_n and l_n can find a solution themselves.

You ask about joining a patrol to talk with these outsiders, like you would with an inter-Clan conflict. Though d_n appreciates the suggestion, {PRONOUN/d_n/subject} {VERB/d_n/remind/reminds} you that your role as a mediator means nothing to the outsiders; they could attack you like they would any other cat on patrol. Disappointed, you return to mulling over potential solutions.

It quickly becomes apparent that l_n and d_n have very different ideas about how to handle these cats: your suggestions are either dismissed by one as too passive, or rejected by the other as too harsh. The two start arguing so intently about the matter, you have to abandon the discussion about the outsiders in favor of mediating d_n's and l_n's bickering.

intro_text: As a mediator, you always felt so helpful and needed down in c_n. Here, however, in the perfect, shining territory of StarClan, arguments and disputes are few and far between. You miss working.
decline_text: You sigh and lay down for a nap... again. Maybe you'll have an entertaining dream.

success_outcomes.text: Perhaps, if you can't find any problems... you can create one! You spend the afternoon sneaking around, mucking up nests and leaving the sparkly fur of other cats at the scenes of the crimes. By the time the evening comes, the StarClan camp has erupted into half a dozen arguments that you're able to bravely solve for everyone.

You decide to take a walk through StarClan's hunting grounds. While you do, you stumble across two birds wrestling over a grub. Aha! You're able to quickly locate a second grub while they're distracted and toss it at the pair. To your delight, one bird grabs your grub and flies off. Conflict solved!

You survey your surroundings until you catch the gaze of a StarClan Clanmate, who isn't looking too happy. A quick conversation confirms what you've been hoping for: they had a small conflict with another cat and they're unsure of how to resolve it. p_l to the rescue!

fail_outcomes.text: You wander around StarClan asking your starry Clanmates if something-- anything-- has been bugging them lately. To your disappointment, every cat is as happy as can be. No fun!

You decide to take a walk and visit each StarClan cat you come across, asking them if there's been anything bugging them lately. Soon enough, one snaps back that the only cat bothering them is you with your nosiness.

intro_text: Today, like any other day in StarClan, is perfect for taking a long, relaxing nap in the clouds... at least, until you're woken up by two apprentices bickering nearby. They both stubbornly want to give a life for the same thing at the next leadership ceremony.
decline_text: You decide their argument is not your business. You regrettably stand from your perfect resting spot and search for an area far out of listening range, deciding your nap is more important an an apprentices' bout.

success_outcomes.text: Disrupted and annoyed by the apprentices' relentless arguing, you suggest they decide who is better at what they wish to grant to the leader through a series of games, and the winner will give the life. This strikes a competitive spark between the two of them, and they run off to do exactly what you suggested. Finally - peace and quiet.

No, they can't grant a leader two lives for the same thing - but why not give two similar lives? Virtues are multifaceted, you explain to the apprentices, and there are many other traits and values similar to the one they're arguing over; they could lay claim to those instead. While they initially grumble about it not being the same, the more verbose of the two begins listing off alternatives, and it's not long before they've both settled on two unique options. The now-delighted apprentices thank you, and you smile as they return to their regular friendly chatter. Job well done!

A mischievous smile creeps onto your face as you suggest your "solution" - if the apprentices can't decide who will give the life, then maybe you should give it instead. As you expected, the idea horrifies the apprentices, and within a few heartbeats they've decided which of them would present the new leader with the life. You purr in amusement to yourself as they scamper off. Typical apprentices - they'd rather compromise than allow some other cat to take a privilege away from them!

You initially try to help the two apprentices find a resolution, but as their bickering grows increasingly petty, your patience wears thinner and thinner. Finally, you snap that if they can't maturely solve this conflict, maybe neither of them should get to present the life to the new leader. After an initial shocked silence, one of the apprentices tentatively suggests to the other that they try the first resolution you offered, and the other hesitantly agrees. You're not sure if they were convinced by your temper, or the idea that they wouldn't get to present any lives to the leader at all - but considering it ended the argument, you don't know that it matters too much.

fail_outcomes.text: You're irritated by the apprentices' nonstop bickering, but are level-headed enough to suggest a solution: the two can have a competition, and whichever cat wins will get to grant the life. Thrilled by the idea, they dart off, and you contentedly curl up to return to your nap... until the apprentices come thundering back, arguing about whether one of them cheated at the game. What does it take to get some peace and quiet around here?

You ponder for a moment, then offer a solution: the two apprentices can grant the new leader one life together. This suggestion only baffles the apprentices, though; one asks if that's even possible, while the other insists that sharing the life would make it less important.

Annoyed that your sunbathing was interrupted by such a pointless argument, you sharply tell them that there's an easy solution - neither of them will present the life to the new leader, because they won't be giving any lives at the ceremony. It's the solution that probably makes the most sense, but you can't help but feel guilty as the apprentices practically wilt in disappointment. Clearly, this was more important to them than the squabbling made it seem.
